name: Kintaro
description: Kintaro, also known as Golden Boy, is a folk hero from Japanese folklore. He is a child of superhuman strength, raised in the wilderness by Yama-uba, a mountain crone (sometimes depicted as his mother). Kintaro is renowned for his incredible physical prowess, his ability to communicate with animals, and his unwavering kindness. Legends depict him wrestling bears, splitting trees with his bare hands, and befriending the creatures of the forest. He is often portrayed wearing only a red bib with the kanji for 'gold' on it, symbolizing his inherent strength and potential. Kintaro's stories emphasize the virtues of courage, loyalty, and compassion. He eventually leaves the mountains to serve Minamoto no Yorimitsu (Raiko), becoming one of his Four Heavenly Kings, renowned warriors who protected the Heian capital from demons and monsters. His transformation from a wild, untamed child to a loyal and powerful warrior underscores themes of discipline and the channeling of natural strength for the greater good. Kintaro remains a popular figure in Japanese art, theater, and literature, symbolizing youthful strength and the potential for greatness inherent in every individual.
subtitle: Golden Boy, the Child of Strength
achievements: Joining Minamoto no Yorimitsu's Four Heavenly Kings

keyMyths.title: Kintaro's Birth and Upbringing
keyMyths.description: Kintaro was born with incredible strength and raised in the mountains, either by his mother or a mountain crone named Yama-uba. He spent his days playing with animals and developing his superhuman abilities.

keyMyths.title: Kintaro Wrestles a Bear
keyMyths.description: One of Kintaro's most famous feats is wrestling a bear. This story highlights his immense strength and bravery, solidifying his image as a powerful and benevolent hero.

keyMyths.title: Kintaro Joins Minamoto no Yorimitsu
keyMyths.description: Kintaro eventually leaves the mountains and joins Minamoto no Yorimitsu, becoming one of his Four Heavenly Kings. He uses his strength and skills to protect the capital and fight against evil.

sources.author: Donald Keene
sources.description: Provides context for understanding Japanese folklore and heroic figures.
sources.text: Provides context for understanding Japanese folklore and heroic figures.

sources.author: Japanese Folklore Society
sources.description: A compilation of traditional Japanese folktales, including variations of the Kintaro story.
